text: Concomitant video pancreatic diversion with duodenal switch and hiatal hernia repair. A totally robotic technique.

The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum using a Veress needle technique. Ports and a liver retractor are placed, revealing a normal-appearing liver and a large hiatal hernia defect with the stomach herniated into the chest.

Marking sutures are placed at 100 cm and 250 cm from the ileocecal valve. The valve at 250 cm is approximated to the anterior abdominal wall. In reverse Trendelenburg position, the robot is docked. Adhesions in the gallbladder fossa are cleared using a harmonic scalpel. The greater curvature of the stomach is mobilized from approximately 4 cm past the pylorus to the angle of His.

The surgeon dissects the cura of the diaphragm both anteriorly and posteriorly, ensuring no injury to the esophagus. The stomach is repositioned, and the proposed staple line is marked with surgical ink. Posterior dissection of the duodenum is performed, followed by division using a tan, 60mm surgical stapler.

The surgeon uses a 32 French bougie to guide the division of the stomach near the antrum with purple 45mm staple loads, initially without seam guards. As the stomach becomes thinner, 45mm staple loads with seam guards are applied more proximally.
The surgeon places two plagiarized sutures of 1-0 Ticron posteriorly using a left thoracic approach. The anterior hiatus defect is approximated with plagiarized 1-0 Ticron sutures.

The surgeon tags the specimen with a single stitch to facilitate extraction. The valve at the 250-centimeter mark is detached from the anterior abdominal wall and approximated to the duodenum. A running back row suture is applied first. Enterotomies are created using hook electrocautery and completed with a harmonic scalpel. The second layer of the anastomosis is completed in a running fashion.

The surgeon places zero muscular stitches to complete a two-layer hand-sewn anastomosis using 3-0 absorbable V-lock sutures. The biliary limb is anastomosed to the ileum at the 100-centimeter mark using a 60-millimeter tan load. The enterotomy is hand-sewn with 3-0 V-lock absorbable suture.

The surgeon closes the mesenteric defect using 3-0 V-lock non-absorbable sutures. The biliary limb is divided near the duodenal ileostomy using a tan 60mm stapler, separating it from the alimentary limb. The stomach is oversewn with absorbable V-lock sutures where reinforcement was not applied.
